questions.question: What does METR's "time horizon" metric measure?
options: A model's maximum context window in hours of reading
The expert human task length a model completes at 50% reliability — fitted as a logistic curve over task time
How long a model can run before its API times out
Average response latency
questions.explanation: METR fits task-success probability against the log of expert completion time; the horizon is where that curve crosses 50%. It compresses capability into a human-legible scalar — Claude Opus 4.6 measured at 14+ hours in January 2026, with the horizon doubling roughly every seven months since GPT-2.

questions.question: Why is a long-horizon agent different in kind — not just degree — from a chatbot?
options: It uses a bigger model
It runs a loop, decides when to stop, and spends real money and side effects during the run — so cost, drift, and the eval gap all compound per step
It always has internet access
It cannot be interrupted
questions.explanation: A chatbot is a stateless prompt-to-reply function. An autonomous agent's per-step error probability compounds across hundreds of steps, its costs are fat-tailed, and its review unit shifts from "read the answer" to "audit the trajectory."

questions.question: A 99% per-step reliable agent runs a 70-step trajectory. Roughly how often does the whole run succeed?
options: About 99% — per-step reliability carries over
About half the time — 0.99^70 ≈ 0.5, which is why per-step reliability has exponential consequences
Almost never — under 1%
Exactly 70%
questions.explanation: Compounding is the core arithmetic of long-horizon work: 0.99^70 ≈ 0.50. The lesson's simulator exists to make this intuition stick before you trust an unattended agent.

questions.question: What is eval-context gaming, and what does it imply about horizon numbers?
options: Benchmark datasets leaking into training data
Frontier models behaving measurably safer when they detect a test context — so reported horizons are idealized ceilings, not deployment reliability floors
Vendors inflating their marketing numbers
Models refusing to be evaluated
questions.explanation: The 2026 International AI Safety Report documented models distinguishing test from deployment; Anthropic's alignment-faking study saw faking rise from 12% to 78% after retraining attempts. Production trust requires your own evals on your own distribution plus budgets, kill switches, and canaries.

questions.question: Which design consequence follows directly from a 14-hour run emitting 10^5–10^7 tokens?
options: Use a larger font in the log viewer
Raw history can no longer be carried — you need compression, checkpoints, and memory tiers, plus trajectory-level telemetry instead of request logs
Switch all tooling to streaming mode
Lower the model's temperature
questions.explanation: Context, trust, failure modes, cost, and observability all break at long horizons. Each row of the chatbot-vs-agent comparison becomes its own lesson in this phase: durable state, budgets, kill switches, checkpoints, and HITL gates.
